summary: DHH argues that when a team achieves productive momentum, the best managerial response is to do nothing and let it run. He contends that professional managers often feel compelled to tinker with processes, teams, and missions because that's their job identity, even when intervention is counterproductive. He credits 37signals' productivity to having very few full-time managers, with most people (including himself and Jason Fried) working in the trenches. The post advocates for management as an occasional hat to wear rather than a permanent role.
key_points: Team momentum is rare and fragile — once achieved, the worst thing a manager can do is intervene
Professional managers are structurally incentivized to tinker even when things are working well
37signals maintains high productivity with only a couple of full-time managers among eighty people
Management should be an occasional role, not a permanent identity — leaders should primarily do the actual work
Having fewer managers eliminates the temptation to fix what isn't broken
money_quotes: The easiest way to mess up a good thing is to mess with it at all.
Sitting on their hands, or at least just polishing the edges, is not a response that comes natural to most professional managers.
Management is not our first love. It's a hat to wear when the occasion calls, then one we happily put back on the shelve when the deed is done.
The vast majority of everyone at 37signals are working in the trenches, and leading from the front.
This relative lack of full-time managers means the temptation to mess with what ain't broken just largely isn't there.
Idle managerial hands run's the devil's workshop.
key_insight: The most productive thing a manager can do when a team has momentum is nothing at all — and companies with fewer full-time managers are less likely to sabotage their own success.
